title: Coffee Granita
subtitle: Strong coffee frozen and scraped into crunchy, bitter crystals
excerpt: Very strong espresso sweetened with sugar, frozen in a shallow tray, and raked with a fork every 30 minutes into loose, crunchy, dark crystals. A Sicilian granita di caffè, served with a drift of whipped cream on top and eaten with a spoon.
sourceNotes: Granita di caffè is a Sicilian speciality as deeply established as the lemon version. It is associated with the morning culture of eastern Sicily, where it is served in bars and cafés with whipped cream (panna) alongside a soft brioche. The technique is identical to the lemon granita, freeze and rake, with strong espresso in place of the citrus syrup.

content.text: Coffee granita is bracing in the way that a well-made espresso is bracing, cold, bitter, sweet, and immediate. The coffee must be strong. Weak coffee produces a granita that tastes of not very much once it is frozen. Use a high dose of espresso powder dissolved in just-boiled water, or brew a very strong stovetop espresso. The sugar level is lower than in the lemon version because coffee already has its own bitterness to balance.

Traditionally served with a spoonful of lightly sweetened whipped cream on top. The cream melts slightly into the cold dark crystals as you eat, which is the intended combination. It is also good plain.

items.name: Espresso powder
items.prepNote: or 400 ml very strong brewed espresso

content.text: You will also need 400 ml of just-boiled water to dissolve the espresso powder and sugar. A wide, shallow metal or plastic tray, about 30 × 20 cm.

Dissolve {{espresso-powder}} of espresso powder and {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar in 400 ml of just-boiled water, stirring until both are completely dissolved. Leave to cool completely. Pour into a wide, shallow tray.

Freeze for 45 minutes until crystals form at the edges. Scrape with a fork, breaking up the frozen edges and mixing them into the liquid centre. Return to the freezer. Repeat every 30 minutes for 2.5, 3 hours until the whole tray is loose, dark, crunchy crystals with no liquid remaining.
Scoop into chilled glasses. Top with a spoonful of {{whipping-cream}} of lightly whipped cream if using. Serve immediately with a long spoon.

items.fix: Leave at room temperature for 15 minutes until the edges soften, then scrape vigorously with a fork and return to the freezer. Repeat the scraping cycle.
items.symptom: Granita froze solid without forming crystals

items.fix: Increase the espresso powder to 5, 6 teaspoons, or use a stovetop espresso brewed at full strength. Freezing mutes flavour, so start stronger than you think you need.
items.cause: Coffee was too weak before freezing
items.symptom: Coffee flavour is very weak once frozen

items.fix: Add 20 g more caster sugar to the mixture. The sugar level can be adjusted to taste before freezing.
items.cause: High ratio of espresso powder, or coffee was over-brewed

items.fix: Use a tray with a maximum liquid depth of 2 cm. Scrape every 25, 30 minutes rather than leaving it longer.
items.cause: Tray was too deep, or scraping intervals were too long
items.symptom: Crystals are large and chunky rather than fine and fluffy

content.text: Granita di caffè is a fixed part of Sicilian daily life. Bars and cafés serve it through the summer as an option to a hot espresso. The standard pairing is coffee granita with lightly whipped cream, granita con panna. It is the usual form in eastern Sicily. The combination makes a breakfast or mid-morning break with no real match elsewhere in Italian food culture. The method is the same as for the lemon version: only the flavour changes. Coffee granita needs no special kit and no cooking beyond dissolving the sugar.
